pattern: Counting how many integers up to a value are divisible by a, b, or c is monotone in the value, so binary search on the answer pins the nth such number using inclusion-exclusion at each step.
hints: Instead of generating ugly numbers one by one, ask how many exist at or below a candidate value.
The count of multiples of a, b, or c up to x comes from inclusion-exclusion over their least common multiples.
Binary search the answer over [1, 2e9]: count = x/a + x/b + x/c - x/lcm(a,b) - x/lcm(a,c) - x/lcm(b,c) + x/lcm(a,b,c); shrink toward the smallest x whose count is at least n.

edge_inputs.description: c is a multiple of a, so overlaps must be removed
n equals one returns the smallest divisor
moderately large n with small divisors

stress.description: random n and divisors within constraints, checked against a counting oracle
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randrange(1, 5000)
    a = rng.randrange(1, 50)
    b = rng.randrange(1, 50)
    c = rng.randrange(1, 50)
    return [n, a, b, c]
stress.note: small divisors keep the brute-force oracle fast while exercising overlaps
